summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 4S as a straightforward upgrade: faster, better camera, and Siri. He spends the bulk of the review on Siri, describing it as a fundamentally different interface paradigm for iOS — open-ended and implicit rather than visual and explicit — and draws a line back to Sculley-era Apple's AI ambitions. He explores the question of why Siri is limited to the 4S and whether third-party integration is even feasible given its centralized design. The camera section emphasizes speed improvements as much as image quality, noting Apple's obsessive engineering focus on shaving tenths of seconds. He concludes that the 4S improves on what was already his favorite Apple product.
key_points: Siri represents a fundamentally different interface paradigm for iOS: open-ended and implicit rather than visual and explicit, marking Apple's first AI-focused ambition since the pre-Jobs era
Third-party Siri integration poses a genuine design problem — unlike the home screen's clean app-icon model, there's no obvious way to add voice commands without conflicts between competing apps
Camera speed improvements (launch time and shot-to-shot time) received enormous engineering attention and matter more in practice than the image quality gains
Siri's restriction to the 4S may be a pragmatic server-load decision as much as a hardware one, given that iOS 5 could reach 100 million users while 4S users would number only 1-2 million
The speech-to-text dictation feature, separate from Siri's assistant capabilities, matches what Gruber considered Android's best feature
money_quotes: Prior to Siri, iOS struck me as being designed to make it easy for us to do things. Siri is designed to do things for us.
There's no way to tell what will work and what won't. You must explore.
Siri is indicative of an AI-focused ambition that Apple hasn't shown since before Steve Jobs returned to the company.
I wouldn't say I can't live without Siri. But I can say that I don't want to.
It's not so obvious how you could add new commands to Siri without potentially stomping on existing ones.
The most profound difference between the 4S and 4 cameras has nothing to do with image quality. It's that you don't have to wait nearly as long.
The Newton was killed by that 'egg freckles' stuff — it never recovered from the public perception that its handwriting recognition wasn't good enough.
key_insight: Siri represents a paradigm shift from iOS's visual, explicit interface to an open-ended, AI-driven one — Apple's most ambitious departure since the Newton era — but its centralized design creates a genuine unsolved problem for third-party integration.
